attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a six-hour history of severe left-sided lower abdominal pain and vomiting. The pain began suddenly while jogging. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She is sexually active with one partner and uses condoms consistently.

On examination, she appears distressed. Her pulse rate is 95/min, blood pressure 125/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 99% on room air, and temperature 37.0°C. Her abdomen is tender to light and deep palpation in the left lower quadrant. Her urine beta-human chorionic gonadotropin test is negative. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an empty uterus with a 5x5 cm simple cyst of the left ovary. There is no fluid in the pouch of Douglas and Doppler shows no flow in the left ovarian artery.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following structures is most likely involved in this presentation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of ovarian torsion - sudden onset unilateral pelvic pain with vomiting, and ultrasound showing an enlarged ovary with absent blood flow. The infundibulopelvic ligament is the key anatomical structure involved as it contains the ovarian vessels and acts as the axis around which the ovary twists. The presence of a 5 cm ovarian cyst serves as a pivot point for the torsion.

The appendix would typically cause right-sided pain and different ultrasound findings. While appendicitis can present with left-sided pain in rare anatomical variants, the ultrasound findings are diagnostic of ovarian pathology.

The round ligament extends from the uterus to the labia majora and can cause pain during pregnancy or with endometriosis, but would not cause the ultrasound findings described.

The cardinal ligament (also known as the transverse cervical ligament) provides lateral support to the cervix and upper vagina. It contains the uterine vessels but is not directly involved in ovarian torsion. Pathology of this ligament would typically present with symptoms related to uterine rather than ovarian issues.

The uterosacral ligament extends from the posterior cervix to the sacrum and provides support to the uterus. It is not directly involved in ovarian torsion and pathology of this ligament typically presents with deep dyspareunia or chronic pelvic pain rather than acute symptoms.

Ovarian torsion is a surgical emergency requiring prompt intervention to preserve ovarian function. The presence of a cyst >5 cm increases the risk, as it provides a fulcrum for rotation. Characteristic ultrasound findings include an enlarged ovary, absent Doppler flow, and sometimes a 'whirlpool sign' of the twisted vascular pedicle. Treatment involves urgent laparoscopy with detorsion and cystectomy if the tissue remains viable.
learningPoints: Ovarian torsion commonly occurs around a cyst >5cm which acts as a pivot point for twisting of the infundibulopelvic ligament.
Ultrasound findings in ovarian torsion include an enlarged ovary, absent Doppler flow, and the 'whirlpool sign'.
Early surgical intervention with laparoscopic detorsion is essential to preserve ovarian function with ovarian torsion.
references: Salvador S, Scott S, Glanc P, Eiriksson L, Jang JH, Sebastianelli A, Dean E. Guideline No. 403: Initial Investigation and Management of Adnexal Masses. J Obstet Gynaecol Can. 2020 Aug;42(8):1021-1029.e3. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32736853/
Huang C, Hong MK, Ding DC. A review of ovary torsion. Ci Ji Yi Xue Za Zhi. 2017;29(3):143-147. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28974907/
Hanson L. Using Ultrasound to Diagnose Ovarian Torsion [online]. 2018. Available at: https://www.volusonclub.net/empowered-womens-health/usingultrasoundtodiagnoseovariantorsion/
Guile SL, Mathai JK. Ovarian Torsion. In: StatPearls [online]. [Updated 2022 Jul 18]. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK560675/
